refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation window. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative uptime / total period; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Verification requires instrumentation of the communication path and logging of all link state changes.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to minimize link downtime.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log and report link availability metrics for post-evaluation analysis.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The vehicle shall enter a safe state upon link loss and remain safe until link restoration.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual independent communication paths (primary and backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; log state transitions.
DP-B: Implement a single high-reliability communication path with error correction and retransmission; rely on network provider SLA.
DP-C: Implement multi-carrier or multi-technology redundancy (e.g., cellular + satellite + mesh); orchestrate failover via a link manager.
DP-D: Implement local autonomy with periodic link synchronization; reduce dependency on continuous link availability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement multi-carrier or multi-technology redundancy (cellular + satellite + mesh) with a centralized link manager that monitors all paths, selects the best available path, and orchestrates seamless failover. Pair with continuous health monitoring, state logging, and safe-state protocols on link loss.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure selection and failover logic; this is acceptable because link availability is inherently a network-level concern.
DP-C introduces dependency on multiple external carriers (cellular, satellite, mesh); this is a managed risk via contractual SLAs and redundancy design.
DP-C does not couple to vehicle propulsion, sensing, or safety logic; those remain independent.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is a decoupled solution at the system level: it solves the link availability problem without imposing constraints on vehicle subsystems. The coupling is horizontal (to network infrastructure) rather than vertical (to vehicle internals).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Multiple independent communication carriers are available at the worksite (cellular, satellite, mesh).
Assumption: Failover latency (< 100 ms) is acceptable for safe vehicle operation.
Risk: Satellite link may have higher latency; mesh network may have limited bandwidth.
Risk: Cost of multi-carrier infrastructure may exceed budget.
Risk: Synchronization of multiple paths may introduce complexity in the link manager.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to specify: (1) failover latency target, (2) minimum bandwidth per path, (3) link health check interval, (4) criteria for path selection (latency, packet loss, signal strength). Consider cost-benefit trade-off: if dual cellular (primary + backup) achieves 0.999 availability at lower cost, prefer DP-B with enhanced monitoring.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication infrastructure, link management, failover orchestration, health monitoring, and state logging. It interfaces with the Vehicle (via Tasking and Telemetry) and with external network carriers (via Network Link). It does not own vehicle control logic or safety decision-making; those remain in Vehicle and Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands from tele-operator (or autonomous system) to vehicle; routed through Teleoperation link manager.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state, sensor data, and diagnostics streamed from vehicle to tele-operator; routed through Teleoperation link manager.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical communication paths (cellular, satellite, mesh); managed by Teleoperation module for redundancy and failover.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Link loss or degradation alerts sent to Safety Services for safe-state enforcement.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. It contains link manager, failover orchestration, health monitoring, and state logging. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services for safe-state coordination.
Vehicle could host link management, but this would couple communication infrastructure to vehicle hardware, reducing modularity. Link availability is a system-level concern, not vehicle-specific.
Safety Services should react to link loss (safe-state enforcement) but should not own link management. Mixing link availability with safety logic violates separation of concerns.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; assigning DP-C here would diffuse responsibility across all subsystems. Not appropriate for detailed implementation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-C (multi-carrier redundancy with link manager). It has clear ownership of communication infrastructure, interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and avoids coupling link availability to vehicle hardware or safety logic.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if it also handles encryption, compression, or protocol translation; recommend separating those concerns into sub-modules.
Risk: Failover latency depends on link manager responsiveness; if latency exceeds vehicle safe-state requirements, coordination with Safety Services must be explicit.
Risk: Multi-carrier management requires contracts and SLAs with external providers; this is an operational/procurement risk, not a technical containment risk.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module scope includes: (1) link health monitoring, (2) failover orchestration, (3) state logging, (4) SafetyAlert signaling. Clarify that Vehicle module does not own link management. Define the SafetyAlert interface contract: what triggers an alert, what is the latency, what actions does Safety Services take?

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (multi-carrier redundancy with link manager) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and feasible. No decoupling violations: the DP solves the link availability problem without imposing unnecessary constraints on vehicle subsystems.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If Teleoperation module does not have clear ownership of failover logic, responsibility may diffuse to Vehicle or Safety Services.
Diffusion risk: If link health monitoring is not centralized in Teleoperation, multiple modules may implement redundant monitoring logic.
Mitigation: Define Teleoperation module scope explicitly; establish clear interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment score (0.95) for DP-C. The module boundary is clear: it owns communication infrastructure and link management, but does not own vehicle control or safety decision-making. External interfaces are well-defined and minimal.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-010 is feasible: instrument the Teleoperation module to log all link state transitions, calculate uptime/total time, and compare against 0.999 threshold. Verification requires access to link health data and evaluation period logs.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≥0.999 link availability. Vehicle module is responsible for safe-state behavior upon link loss (coordinated via SafetyAlert). Safety Services is responsible for enforcing safe-state policies.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify failover latency target (e.g., < 100 ms) and link health check interval (e.g., every 1 second).
Define SafetyAlert interface contract: what triggers an alert, what is the latency guarantee, what actions does Safety Services take?

Establish acceptance criteria for link availability measurement: evaluation period duration, measurement granularity (e.g., per second, per minute), and handling of transient link glitches (e.g., < 1 second).
Confirm that Teleoperation module has access to all required network carriers (cellular, satellite, mesh) and that SLAs are in place.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, redundancy/failover, metrics logging, safe-state behavior). DP-C is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. The next level of decomposition is necessary to detail the link manager, failover logic, health monitoring, and safe-state coordination. Proceed with functional decomposition of sub-FRs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to minimize link downtime.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log and report link availability metrics for post-evaluation analysis.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The vehicle shall enter a safe state upon link loss and remain safe until link restoration.

assumptions: Multiple independent communication carriers (cellular, satellite, mesh) are available at the worksite.
Failover latency < 100 ms is acceptable for safe vehicle operation.
Link health can be monitored continuously without significant overhead.
External network carriers provide SLAs that, when combined, achieve ≥0.999 availability.
Tele-operator and autonomous systems can tolerate brief link interruptions (< 100 ms) during failover.
open_risks: Cost of multi-carrier infrastructure may exceed budget; cost-benefit trade-off with dual-cellular solution needed.
Satellite link latency may be too high for real-time teleoperation; latency requirements must be validated.
Mesh network coverage at worksite is uncertain; coverage survey required.
Synchronization of multiple paths in link manager may introduce complexity; detailed design review needed.
Safe-state behavior upon link loss must be coordinated with Safety Services; interface contract must be explicit.
evidence_needed: Network carrier SLAs and availability data for cellular, satellite, and mesh at target worksites.
Latency measurements for each carrier and failover latency targets from vehicle control requirements.
Cost estimates for multi-carrier infrastructure vs. dual-cellular solution.
Worksite network coverage surveys (cellular, satellite, mesh).
Vehicle safe-state requirements and safe-state transition latency limits.
Detailed design of link manager, failover orchestration, and health monitoring logic.
Test plan for link availability verification (evaluation period, measurement granularity, acceptance criteria).
